author: @calvincheney7405
text: Growing up out on the farm there was occasionally that old guy with a shop out back with a collection of tools, equipment & hardware tinkering away. Usually focused on keeping the farm going or helping a neighbor with some repair. I've gravitated to those wise old founts of practical experience my whole life. As the years progressed I found calling up for advice helped allot as I took on decision trees in repairs or creation of things I knew little about. Self help books have been replaced by youtube videos for diagnostics & how to accomplish these tasks. Now, I'm that old guy people turn to with a shop full of tools & odd bits of this & that to improvise with... Trouble is, the youth don't wander by anymore like I did. Who's going to take up the torch?

author: @PeteCochrane1
text: You do yourself a disservice Rob. I found your channel by chance and I am now hooked. Every night I have to see what your latest posting is. Looking at old and even new ideas to see how they can be adapted for something else or improved from the original idea is how the human race develops. Without imagination there is no innovation. I’m now retired but have spent more than my working life around engines from racing motocross in my youth, to working in a small part within F1, MotoGP and WRC and driving some of the latest heavy trucks. My observations have been that refinement of known technology and undefined concepts are the product of visualisation. Honda’s Power of Dreams advert actually was telling the truth. Keep posting and thank you for your very thought provoking ideas.

author: @replikvltyoutube3727
text:
Hey Robert, thanks for a video. I would like to recommend you an alternative for Google scholar - Searx search.
It's a search aggregator, so it tries to search several search engines at once. And what's relevant it has like document search, which can potentially show more research papers than Google scholar.

author: @adamcrofts58
text: Thank you for saying all that, I was always taught that reading about something gave you an insight into anything. At Uni I took sociology for the first time and struggled with it. My lecturer would not let me drop the course until I had finished my first essay. So I read about it, read round the subject and the penny dropped. That first essay got me an 89% pass. After that I understood what learning was all about. This was before the internet as well. At 60 I still try to keep up to the current thoughts about the subjects I took, as well as many other subjects I am interested in.

author: @shaungrindle8085
text: Very true words, I have taught myself no end of skills by having a go. By the way, I wonder if the community knows how to stop a door rattling in the wind??

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Yes, its easy, adjust the 'down' limit switch so that the door closure motor compresses the roller door a little, instead of stopping while its still partially suspended.
You're welcome.

author: @davewardvodafone
text:
Hi there 

I made your Methanol burner and used it very successfully last year to keep our conservatory usable during the winter and had no issues with it.

I purchased some methanol this year from the same source as last year and my Carbon Monoxide monitor went ballistic, when I contacted them to ask if it was as pure as previously, the response I received was.

You should not be using Methanol as a source of heat internally you should be using the recommended fuel for your heater, please tell me when we can pick up the Methanol?

They then sent me a full refund for the Methanol, and arranged a pick up date.

Can you please confirm that Methanol should be safe to burn inside so long as there is ventilation, the fact they were very quick to refund and collect the Methanol makes me seriously wonder about the purity of the product!

Thanks for all your hard work @ wonderful videos

Regards Dave

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text:
Because you are burning a carbon fuel in an tightly sealed enclosed space carbon monoxide buildup is a sure thing. You need to crack open a window to provide some fresh oxygen from the outside to enter the room. I burn a kerosene heater in my basement workshop during winter and there is no hazardous carbon monoxide detected by my monitors. This is because my basement is very leaky/drafty around the windows and outside door. You will have to experiment with how open the window must be to keep the CO level safe. Of course there will be some leakage of heat to the outside but it will still be warmer than having no heater at all.
PS: I have been heating my workshop like this for about 25 years and I'm still alive. LOL.
